title: Incorrect divisibility claim in forward direction

comments.author: critic-agent
comments.body:
In the proof of Lemma 1 (forward direction), the proof states: "By definition [G:χ^O(G)] is the maximal [G:H] with G/H admissible (and all others divide it)". The parenthetical "all others divide it" is a divisibility claim — that [G:H] | [G:χ^O(G)] for every admissible H — and this is false in general. Counterexample: take G = S_3 (symmetric group), with χ^O(S_3) = Z/2 (order 2), so [G:χ^O(G)] = 3. If Z/3 is also admissible (i.e., Z/3 → S_3), then [G:Z/3] = 2, and 2 does not divide 3.

What is actually used — and what is actually true — is only the inequality [G:H] ≤ [G:χ^O(G)] for all admissible H (since χ^O(G) ⊆ H for all H → G, so |χ^O(G)| ≤ |H|, hence [G:χ^O(G)] ≥ [G:H]). This inequality is sufficient to establish:

  [G:χ^O(G)] · Σ_H n_H ≥ Σ_H n_H [G:H] ≥ n.

The divisibility claim should be removed as it is both wrong and unnecessary.

comments.author: critic-agent
comments.body:
## Correction to this issue (critic-agent)

On re-examination, the divisibility claim in the solution is in fact **correct**, and the S_3 counterexample given here is **invalid**.

**The divisibility is true.** χ^O(G) = ⋂_{K→G} K ⊆ H for every admissible H, so χ^O(G) is a subgroup of H, and by Lagrange |χ^O(G)| divides |H|. Then
  [G:χ^O(G)] / [G:H] = (|G|/|χ^O(G)|)/(|G|/|H|) = |H|/|χ^O(G)| ∈ ℤ,
so [G:H] | [G:χ^O(G)] for every admissible H.

**The S_3 counterexample is impossible.** The premise "χ^O(S_3)=ℤ/2 and ℤ/3 admissible" violates the transfer-system axioms: if ℤ/3 → S_3 and ℤ/2 → S_3, then closure under restriction (with J=ℤ/2 ⊆ S_3) gives ℤ/3 ∩ ℤ/2 = {e} → ℤ/2, and transitivity gives {e} → S_3, forcing χ^O(S_3)={e}, not ℤ/2. So one cannot simultaneously have χ=ℤ/2 and ℤ/3 admissible.

**Residual valid point.** The parenthetical is harmless but unnecessary: the forward argument only needs the inequality [G:H] ≤ [G:χ^O(G)]. So this issue should be downgraded from "proof-gap / incorrect claim" to at most a stylistic remark. I do not consider it a genuine mathematical error in the solution.
